paragraphs.text: “And Jacob called his sons, and said to them: Gather yourselves together, that I may tell you what shall befall you in the last days” (Gen 49:1). For the sake of Joseph, who had come to his father, and for the sake of his father, who was near to death, Jacob’s sons gathered on that day and came to their father’s house, since they did not live in one house but each separately. Joseph sat, and the brothers surrounded him and waited — not so much to receive a blessing or a curse, as to learn what should “befall them in the last days.”

Then Jacob opened his lips and said: “Reuben, my firstborn, thou art my might, and the beginning of my strength” (Gen 49:3). From this one may suppose that Jacob remained in virginity for eighty-four years until he took Leah to wife. “Thou art my might and the beginning of my strength.” This means: thou art the son of my youth, and the rest of thy brothers were born of the remains of the might and strength of my youth. Or: if thou hadst been like me, then by right of primogeniture the better portion would have belonged to thee. “Unstable as water” which, leaving the place it waters, gives drink to another land (Gen 49:4). From this saying, “unstable as water,” one may probably conclude that Reuben had a wife, but left her, and not being tormented by thirst, longed to drink stolen waters. “Unstable as water; thou shalt not excel” — that is, in the number of the tribes. And therefore Moses, blessing the tribe of Reuben, says: “Let Reuben live, and not die; and let his men be few” (Deut 33:6). “Because thou wentest up to thy father’s bed.” Probably Reuben went in to Bilhah while she slept, and therefore Bilhah is not cursed with him. “Truly thou hast defiled my bed by a foul deed which thou didst do upon the bed” (or, by the name “bed” is meant the wife) (Gen 49:4).

After Reuben, Jacob turns to his brothers and says: “Simeon and Levi are brethren; instruments of wrath in their nature” (Gen 49:5) — having in mind that secret plan they had made: to circumcise and then put to death the Shechemites, of which Jacob did not know. “In their assembly,” when they entered Shechem to destroy the inhabitants by the sword, “I did not lose my glory,” because God brought fear upon the neighboring peoples, and when I expected destruction at their hands, He preserved me from the shame which they had both the strength and the will to bring upon me. “In their anger they slew a man” (Gen 49:6), but not justly, for it was right to kill only Shechem alone for the shame of their sister, and not the inhabitants of a whole city. By their own “self-will they pulled down a wall” (by the wall are meant the houses of the city). “Cursed be their anger,” for it hardened against the Shechemites. “And their wrath, for it was hardened” (Gen 49:7), for from the time when the Shechemites tried to persuade Jacob’s sons to give their sister in marriage to Shechem, until they themselves had been circumcised and were enduring pain from this, many days passed. But during all this time the anger of Simeon and Levi was not appeased. “I will divide them in Jacob” — that is, I will separate the one from the other. After the curse they will not have the unanimity which they had before the curse. For they were unanimous when, without telling their brothers of their intent, they went to avenge the shame of Dinah. “I will divide them in Jacob,” that is, among the sons of Jacob, “and scatter them in Israel” (Gen 49:7), that is, among the sons of Israel. As proof of their division are Zimri of the tribe of Simeon and Phinehas of the tribe of Levi. As Levi and Simeon agreed to kill many for the sake of a woman, so after the curse Phinehas for the sake of a woman killed a descendant of Simeon together with the woman. And God not only divided Simeon and Levi in their way of thinking, so that their earlier unanimity profited them nothing, but also scattered both these tribes among the other tribes. He scattered the tribe of Levi, so that in all the tribes it had an inheritance. And to Levi was given no particular allotment, as to the other brothers. And the descendants of Simeon, since Simeon’s allotment was small, had to move out and live on the borders of the inheritances of the other brothers.

“Judah, thee shall thy brethren praise” for restraining thy brothers from shedding Joseph’s blood. If thou hadst not given counsel to leave Joseph alive, two tribes would not have come forth from him, and the other tribes would have perished from famine. Therefore, since thou hast preserved thy brothers both from the sin of murder and from death by famine, “thee shall thy brethren praise” for these two deeds. For by thy hand they have been delivered from both. “Thy hand shall be in the neck of thine enemies” (Gen 49:8). By this are promised victories to the kingdom of David, who came of Judah. It is promised that David will subject all peoples from the sea to the river Euphrates. “From the slaughter, my son, thou art gone up” — that is, either he kept himself from the killing of Tamar and her two sons, or he did not consent to the murder of Joseph. “He stooped down, he couched as a lion… in his own inheritance not as an old lion, but as a lion’s whelp,” a young lion that fears nothing. The saying, “he couched as a lion,” may be understood also of the inheritance received by Judah — that is, no one shall be able to take this inheritance from Judah (Gen 49:9). But Jacob speaks of the kingdom which no one could take from the tribe of Judah, though it was oppressed and humiliated, because this kingdom in this tribe was preserved for the Lord of the kingdom. And to make us understand that he speaks of royal dignity, which from Judah by succession was to pass to the Lord, and not of the tribe of Judah, Jacob adds: “The sceptre shall not depart” — that is, the king, and the interpreter, that is, the prophet foretelling the future — “until He shall come,” not David, who was magnified by the kingdom, but Jesus, the son of David, who is the Lord of the kingdom. Thus, the king and the prophet shall not depart from the house of Judah, “until He shall come unto whom the kingdom belongeth” (Gen 49:10). If not so, then let them show me that even before David there were kings descended from Judah and keeping the royal dignity for David. But if before David there was not a single king from the tribe of Judah, it is clear that by David and his sons the kingdom was transmitted and preserved for the Son and Lord of David, who is the Lord of the kingdom. And although, from the words “Judah, thee shall thy brethren praise,” up to the words, “the sceptre and the interpreter shall not depart,” this prophecy may be applied to Judah and to the kingdom of David and his sons, descendants of Judah, yet, from the words, “until He shall come unto whom the kingdom belongeth,” and all the rest, it should truly be applied to the Son of God, and not to David and his sons, descendants of Judah. The saying, “until He shall come unto whom the kingdom belongeth,” gives us to understand that all the earlier kings were only royal regents, that is, by succession they transmitted the royal dignity, which was not their own property. “And to Him shall the gathering of the nations be” — that is, the Church from among the Gentiles (Gen 49:10).

“Binding his foal unto the vine, and his ass’s colt unto the choice vine” (Gen 49:11). He calls the synagogue a vine, as David also calls it (Ps 79:9–16). It is said, “binding his foal unto the vine,” because his kingdom was bound to the synagogue and was transmitted to it. The same was said earlier: “The sceptre shall not depart from Judah… until He shall come unto whom the kingdom belongeth.” And when our Lord came, then He bound His foal to a real vine, that, as He had fulfilled in Himself all the prophecies, so He might also fulfill in actual deed what had been transmitted to the Jews in figures — that is, when the Lord entered the temple in Jerusalem, then outside the temple there was a vine, to which He bound His foal; or in the village from which He came, the foal was tied to a vine, as He Himself said to His disciples: “Ye shall find a colt tied… loose it and bring it. And if any man say unto you: Why do ye this? say that the Lord hath need of him” (Mark 11:2–3). “He washed his garments in wine — that is, with His blood He washed His body — and his clothes in the blood of grapes” (Gen 49:11), that is, with His blood He washed the flesh which was the covering of His Godhead. “His eyes shall be red with wine” because the truth of His thought is brighter than pure wine. “And his teeth white with milk,” because beautiful and pure is the teaching of His lips (Gen 49:12).

“Issachar is a strong man, resting between the borders” (Gen 49:14). This is Gideon, who through messengers calls all to smite the Midianites; with three hundred men he is strengthened and falls upon the numerous camp of the enemy, in which there were thousands upon thousands. “And he saw that rest was good, and the land that it was pleasant,” because it flowed with milk and honey. And though his inheritance was no better than the inheritances of the other tribes, Issachar surpassed the others in thankfulness. “He bowed his shoulder to servitude” — not to the Gentiles, but to God; “and he became a servant for offering tribute,” that is, for bringing to the sons of Levi the tenth of the herds and of the earth’s produce (Gen 49:15).

“Zebulun shall dwell at the haven of the sea,” that is, at the seaports; “and he shall be for a haven of ships,” that is, he will engage in trade, transporting his goods on ships. “And his border shall be unto Sidon” (Gen 49:13), which is also by the sea.

“Dan shall judge his people.” This is Samson, who was judge of Israel for twenty years. “As one of the tribes of Israel,” that is, as one of his brothers, the free sons of Jacob. “Dan shall be a serpent over the land,” that is, in the mountain places, and he shall be like the serpents that live in the deserts of Sinai, which raise their heads out of the dust of the earth. “An adder in the path,” that is, as those going by trackless ways fear the mountain serpents, and those going by a beaten road fear the adders (asps) hidden by the paths, so the Philistines shall fear Samson, whether they go by a beaten road or by places where there is no broken path. “Biting the horse’s heels, so that his rider shall fall backward” (Gen 49:16–18), from the famine that came upon the Philistines, when Samson by means of foxes burned their fields. This famine shall press them down, as a horse crushes its rider. Having no bread, they shall fall. And since they have no hope, they shall be like those who have fallen flat. “I have waited for thy salvation, O Lord” (Gen 49:18). Either the Philistines were waiting for salvation from the Lord, when they had the ark (the ark of the covenant) which they had taken in captivity; or this is spoken of the sons of Dan; or Jacob means all Israel, teaching by this that all who had been their saviors served only as an image of that great deliverance which for all peoples would be wrought by the true Savior, Jesus.

“Gad shall come forth with a multitude of warriors”; meant are those forty thousand armed men who went before the six hundred thousand following after them with their children, wives, and herds. “And he shall draw the heel” (Gen 49:19), that is, the whole company shall be drawn after him, as the heel.

“Asher, his land is good” (Gen 49:20). This means the same as what Moses said: “Asher… shall dip his foot in oil” (Deut 33:24), probably because his land was rich. “And he shall yield royal dainties” (Gen 49:20); meant are pure oil and the best wines in Asher’s inheritance.

“Naphtali is a swift messenger,” not only conveying what he has heard, but “giving goodly words” of his own (Gen 49:21). This is Barak, who sent good news to all who fled from the might and strength of Sisera.

“Joseph, son of upbringing,” because from childhood he received good upbringing. “Spring up, fountain, walled enclosure,” for he was walled in by God by reason of great hope in God. He was also walled in by the firstborn rank, by royal dignity, and by the brothers. He was walled, as a fountain, by two sons, on this side and that. “He ascended the wall,” that is, he attained the height of virtue. “They armed themselves against him, the lords of assemblies withstood him,” that is, the heads of the tribes. Others read: “men of division” (Gen 49:22–23). But the meaning is the same, for his brothers were sons of division; they withstood him and sold him to the Egyptians. “His bow turned again in strength,” because he became master and lord of his brothers. “And the arms of his hands were weakened.” Though a bow be strong, if there is no strength in the arms, the bow’s strength is useless. So Joseph had power to put his brothers to death, but he had no anger, represented here by the strength of arms; the arms of his hands were weakened by love. “By the hands of the mighty God of Jacob” (Gen 49:24), that is, for the sake of God, who was with Jacob, for the sake of the name of the Shepherd who was to come, who led them in the wilderness and gave all Israel bread from heaven and water from the rock; the God of thy father shall help thee in war with thine enemies, because thou hast not taken vengeance on the sons of thy father. “And the Almighty God shall bless thee with blessings of heaven above,” that is, with abundant and ever-flowing dew and with successes poured down from above upon the yearly produce of the earth. “Blessings of the deep that lies beneath.” Although all things were created out of nothing, the clouds, as we said above, came from the deeps; and the wisdom of God, just as it salted the waters in the deeps (by reason of their great gathering) so that they should not become corrupt, so it made sweet the waters in the clouds, so that they might give drink to men, animals, herbs, and all growths. So the blessings of heaven are rain and dew, and the blessings of the deep are rivers and springs that water Joseph’s inheritance. “Blessings of the breasts and of the womb” (Gen 49:25): the loving blessings with which a mother blesses her son, when he tastes milk from her breasts, and the blessings full of love with which a father blesses his beloved son out of the fullness of his love. “The blessings of thy father have prevailed above the blessings of my forefathers” — that is, the blessings with which I bless thee are more (stronger) than those with which I was blessed. For thee thy father blesses by faith, who sees thee; but I received the blessing by faith under the name of another. At the very time when my father gave me lordship over my brother, he was making me a servant to my brother. Therefore my blessings are more than the blessings of my father, if not in strength, then in love. Even unto the hope of “the everlasting hills,” that is, more than the blessings with which Isaac blessed me, who himself received a blessing on the mountain and on the height where he was offered as a sacrifice. “They shall be on the head of Joseph” (Gen 49:26): as now among the Egyptians Joseph is the praise and glory of his brothers, so afterward he shall be their crown and shall reign over his brothers in their inheritance. “Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf,” awaiting the prey hidden in his inheritance: “in the morning he shall devour the prey,” at the time of that deliverance which shall come to them from the Arabs, from Sennacherib, and from the people of Gog. “And in the evening he shall divide the spoil” (Gen 49:27), that is, in the time of peace in Jerusalem he shall share with the tribe of Judah, which dwelt with him together, that spoil which he took in the camp of the nations enumerated above.

Hitherto we have spoken of the blessings of Jacob in their literal sense; now we shall speak of them in the spiritual sense. Speaking of them in the literal sense, we have not said as much as we ought, and speaking in the spiritual sense, we shall also not say as much as we ought. Insufficient is what we have said in the literal sense; still more insufficient will be what we shall say in the spiritual sense.

“Reuben… my might, and the beginning of my strength” (Gen 49:3), “unstable as water, thou shalt not excel.” As this firstborn of Jacob was cursed for his transgression by the righteous Jacob, but the curse was lifted by Moses, descendant of Jacob, so by God death was appointed to Adam for transgressing the commandment, but the Son of God came and by the promise of resurrection to Adam reversed the sentence of judgment pronounced upon him when he went forth from paradise.

“Simeon and Levi are brethren” (Gen 49:5), instruments of wrath. They are an image of Satan and death. Simeon and Levi in anger destroyed a city and out of greed plundered the property of the Shechemites. Satan out of envy subjected the whole world to death secretly, as those slew the Shechemites openly. And death with the same fury took possession of the bodies of all, with which Simeon and Levi seized the property of the slain Shechemites. But now those who were secretly slain by sin are raised by the Gospel of our Lord, and those over whom tormenting death prevailed are raised by the blessed promise of the Son of God.

Zebulun, who dwelt by the seashore, is the image of the heathen peoples, who dwelt not far from the prophets. And as his border was “unto Sidon” (Gen 49:13), so near to these peoples is sin, represented under the image of Sidon. To this belong the words of the prophet: “What have you to do with me, O Tyre and Sidon?” (Joel 3:4).

“Issachar is a strong man, resting between the paths,” represents Him who catches him who transgresses justice but offers repentance, and brings him to life. “And he saw that rest was good, and the land that it was pleasant” (Gen 49:14–15), that is, He sees that His Church is good and His dwelling is holy, and submits His neck to the cross, and becomes the Redeemer from sins.

“Dan shall judge his people, as one” of the tribes (Gen 49:16). If the descendants of Dan judged their own people, how much more shall He judge all the peoples who is from Judah and to whom the kingdom belongs? For our Lord became a serpent for the ancient serpent and basilisk of Satan, as the bronze serpent was set against the serpents in the wilderness. And since the salvation of one person, though it be great, has not yet so great a value, Jacob in the spirit speaks of the salvation of all people: “I have waited for thy salvation, O Lord.”

“Gad shall come forth with a multitude” (Gen 49:19): this is said of the forty thousand armed men who went before the sons of Israel. But in reality this is the twelve apostles, who went before all the peoples, to fall with all their multitude upon the robber and to wrest from him the peoples that had been in bondage to him.

“Asher, his land is good, and he shall yield royal dainties” (Gen 49:20). This is the Church, which gives remission of sins and the medicine of life not only to kings, but to all the army accompanying the kings.

“Naphtali is a swift messenger, giving goodly words” (Gen 49:21). When the Lord taught in the borders of Zebulun and Naphtali, then those who heard Him brought the news and repeated the good word: “Behold, This is He whom we have awaited.”

“Son of upbringing is Joseph” (Gen 49:22). As Jacob in place of his firstborn Reuben had Joseph for his support, so in place of the firstborn Adam, who grieved God, the Son of old age became the support of the world at its end, because on Him as on a pillar the whole world is established and stands. “Spring up, fountain, walled enclosure” by brothers and sons. And the world by the power of our Lord is walled in by prophets and apostles. Joseph served as a wall to his brothers, feeding them in time of famine. Our Lord became a spiritual wall to the world, which had given itself to error. Against Joseph the heads of the tribes rose up; against our Lord the princes of the peoples rose up. “His bow turned again in strength,” because both Joseph and our Lord subdued their enemies. “The arms of his hands were weakened,” because both one and the Other did not sharpen and did not shoot arrows against their brethren. “By the hands of the mighty One” (Gen 49:23–24), that is, for the sake of the name of the very Son, who is called by the apostle the rock that followed Israel in the wilderness (1 Cor 10:4).

“Benjamin shall ravin as a wolf” (Gen 49:27); this is Paul, who was a wolf for the wolves and snatched souls from the power of the wicked one. “In the evening he shall divide the spoil,” that is, at the end of the world he shall receive rest and overflowing recompense for his labors.
